name: Newton's First Law of Motion
statement: An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity in a straight line, unless acted upon by a net external force.
description: Defines inertia and establishes the concept of inertial reference frames. Implies that a force is required to change the state of motion, not to maintain it.

name: Newton's Second Law of Motion
statement: The net force acting on an object is equal to the rate of change of its momentum; for constant mass, this equals mass times acceleration.
description: Provides the quantitative relationship between force, mass, and acceleration. Allows prediction of motion when forces are known, and is the foundation of classical dynamics.

name: Newton's Third Law of Motion
statement: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction: when one body exerts a force on a second body, the second body exerts an equal force in the opposite direction on the first.
description: Establishes that forces always occur in pairs. Critical for understanding rocket propulsion, walking, and all contact interactions.

name: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
statement: Every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
description: Unified terrestrial and celestial mechanics, explaining both falling objects and planetary orbits. Superseded by general relativity for extreme gravitational fields.

name: Hooke's Law
statement: The force required to extend or compress a spring is proportional to the displacement from its equilibrium position, provided the elastic limit is not exceeded.
description: Describes elastic deformation in springs and materials. The basis of spring mechanics, vibration analysis, and the theory of elasticity in solids.

name: Conservation of Momentum
also_known_as: Law of Conservation of Linear Momentum
statement: The total linear momentum of a closed system remains constant if no net external force acts on it.
description: A consequence of Newton's third law and translational symmetry. Governs collisions, explosions, and rocket propulsion. Universally valid across all physics domains.

name: Bernoulli's Principle
statement: For an ideal fluid in steady flow, an increase in velocity occurs simultaneously with a decrease in pressure or potential energy.
description: Expresses conservation of energy for fluid flow. Explains lift on aircraft wings, the operation of carburetors, and the curve of spinning balls.

name: Archimedes' Principle
statement: A body immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.
description: Explains why objects float or sink, and underpins the design of ships, submarines, and balloons. One of the earliest quantitative physical laws.

name: Coulomb's Law
statement: The electrostatic force between two point charges is proportional to the product of their charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
description: The foundational law of electrostatics, analogous in form to Newton's law of gravitation. Leads to Gauss's law and is a special case of Maxwell's equations.

name: Ohm's Law
statement: The current through a conductor between two points is directly proportional to the voltage across those two points, provided temperature and other conditions remain constant.
description: The fundamental relationship in electrical circuits. Defines electrical resistance and underlies all circuit analysis in electronics and electrical engineering.

name: Faraday's Law of Induction
statement: A changing magnetic flux through a circuit induces an electromotive force (EMF) in the circuit proportional to the rate of change of the flux.
description: Explains how electric generators and transformers work. Together with Ampere's law it forms the core of electromagnetic induction. One of Maxwell's four equations.

name: Gauss's Law for Electricity
statement: The total electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the total electric charge enclosed divided by the permittivity of free space.
description: One of Maxwell's four equations; equivalent to Coulomb's law for static fields. Used to calculate electric fields for highly symmetric charge distributions.

name: Maxwell's Equations
statement: Four coupled partial differential equations describing how electric and magnetic fields are generated by charges, currents, and each other, predicting the existence of electromagnetic waves.
description: Unified electricity, magnetism, and optics; predicted electromagnetic radiation and its speed (the speed of light). The starting point for all classical electrodynamics and photonics.

name: Stefan-Boltzmann Law
statement: The total energy radiated per unit surface area of a black body per unit time is proportional to the fourth power of the black body's absolute temperature.
description: Describes the total power output of a blackbody radiator. Essential in astrophysics for determining stellar luminosities and in thermal engineering.

name: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
statement: If two thermodynamic systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.
description: Establishes temperature as a meaningful and transitive physical property, providing the logical basis for thermometry and the concept of thermal equilibrium.

name: First Law of Thermodynamics
discoverer: Julius Robert von Mayer / James Prescott Joule / Hermann von Helmholtz
statement: The total energy of an isolated system is conserved; energy can be transformed from one form to another but cannot be created or destroyed.
description: The thermodynamic expression of energy conservation. Establishes the internal energy of a system and constrains all physical and chemical processes.

name: Second Law of Thermodynamics
statement: The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases over time; natural processes spontaneously evolve toward states of higher entropy.
description: Defines the direction of time, the impossibility of a perfect heat engine, and the tendency of systems toward disorder. Underpins refrigeration, heat engines, and chemical equilibrium.

name: Third Law of Thermodynamics
statement: As the temperature of a system approaches absolute zero, the entropy of a perfect crystal approaches a constant minimum value (conventionally zero).
description: Establishes an absolute reference for entropy and implies that absolute zero is unattainable in a finite number of steps. Essential for low-temperature physics and cryogenics.

name: Planck's Law
statement: The spectral energy density of electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body in thermal equilibrium at temperature T is given by a specific distribution over frequency.
description: Introduced the quantum hypothesis (energy quantization E = hν), resolving the ultraviolet catastrophe and launching quantum mechanics.

name: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
statement: It is impossible to simultaneously determine the exact position and exact momentum of a particle; the product of the uncertainties in position and momentum is always at least ħ/2.
description: A fundamental feature of quantum mechanics, not a measurement limitation but an intrinsic property of nature. Also applies to energy and time: ΔE·Δt ≥ ħ/2.

name: Schrödinger Equation
statement: The time evolution of a quantum system is governed by a linear partial differential equation whose solution is the wave function describing the probability amplitude of the system's states.
description: The central equation of non-relativistic quantum mechanics. Its solutions (wave functions) yield all measurable quantum properties of atoms, molecules, and materials.

name: Pauli Exclusion Principle
statement: No two identical fermions (particles with half-integer spin) can occupy the same quantum state simultaneously within a quantum system.
description: Explains the structure of the periodic table, the stability of matter, electron shell configurations, and the behavior of neutron stars and white dwarfs.

name: Special Theory of Relativity
statement: The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for all observers regardless of their relative motion.
description: Unified space and time into spacetime, showed mass-energy equivalence (E=mc²), and introduced time dilation and length contraction. Foundational for particle physics and GPS.

name: General Theory of Relativity
statement: Gravity is not a force but the curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy; objects follow geodesics (straight paths) through curved spacetime.
description: Replaced Newton's law of gravitation for extreme fields. Predicted gravitational waves, black holes, the Big Bang, and gravitational lensing; all subsequently confirmed.

name: Doppler Effect
statement: The observed frequency of a wave is shifted relative to the emitted frequency when the source and observer are in relative motion; the frequency increases when approaching and decreases when receding.
description: Applies to sound, light, and all waves. Used in radar guns, medical ultrasound, astronomical redshift measurement, and the discovery of the expanding universe.
